questions.stem: A 68-year-old man attends for a routine health check. He has no urinary symptoms. Digital rectal examination (DRE) reveals a firm, nodular prostate. PSA is 12 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A firm, nodular prostate on DRE with elevated PSA in an asymptomatic man is classic for prostate cancer. Benign prostatic hyperplasia typically presents with smooth enlargement and lower PSA.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with hesitancy, frequency, and a weak urinary stream for 6 months. DRE shows a mildly enlarged, smooth prostate. PSA is 6 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Smooth prostatic enlargement with mild lower urinary tract symptoms and moderately elevated PSA is most consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia. Prostatic cancer often presents with nodularity or irregularity on DRE.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with acute inability to pass urine for 12 hours. He has had mild urinary symptoms for months. DRE reveals a hard, irregular prostate. PSA is 45 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute urinary retention due to benign prostatic hyperplasia
questions.explanation: Hard, irregular prostate with very high PSA and acute retention is highly suggestive of prostate cancer causing outflow obstruction, rather than benign causes.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with severe lower back pain and bilateral leg weakness. He has no urinary symptoms. MRI shows multiple osteoblastic metastases in the lumbar spine. PSA is 120 ng/mL. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Osteoblastic (sclerotic) bone metastases with a very high PSA are diagnostic of metastatic prostate cancer. Multiple myeloma typically causes lytic lesions.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old asymptomatic man attends a routine health check. His PSA is 4.8 ng/mL. Digital rectal examination (DRE) is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend multiparametric MRI as the first-line investigation for men with a raised PSA and suspected prostate cancer. Repeat PSA may be appropriate if PSA is borderline but a single result >4.0 ng/mL with normal DRE warrants MRI. Biopsy is reserved for MRI-visible lesions.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with lower urinary tract symptoms. DRE reveals a hard, irregular prostate. PSA is 12 ng/mL. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Although MRI is recommended before biopsy for local staging and targeting, a tissue diagnosis is required to confirm prostate cancer. In this scenario with a high clinical suspicion (hard, irregular prostate and raised PSA), biopsy is the confirmatory test. MRI would be performed prior to biopsy but does not confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a PSA of 4.2 ng/mL has a free-to-total PSA ratio of 0.10. How should this result be interpreted?
options: Strongly suggestive of benign prostatic hyperplasia
Suggests an increased risk of prostate cancer
Indicates a low probability of prostate cancer
questions.explanation: A free-to-total PSA ratio <0.12 is associated with an increased risk of prostate cancer. It is not diagnostic but raises suspicion, prompting further investigation such as MRI. A ratio >0.25 is more typical of benign disease.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with a PSA of 25 ng/mL. He is asymptomatic and DRE reveals a firm nodule. He has had no prior prostate investigation. What is the next investigation recommended by NICE?
options: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: NICE guidance (CG175) states that multiparametric MRI should be offered before prostate biopsy in all men with suspected prostate cancer, regardless of PSA level. This allows risk stratification and targeted biopsies. PSA velocity is not recommended for decision-making.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a previous negative transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy 18 months ago now has a rising PSA from 4.0 to 6.5 ng/mL. DRE is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends that men with a prior negative biopsy but persistently raised or rising PSA should have a multiparametric MRI before considering re-biopsy. MRI can identify lesions that may have been missed and guide targeted biopsies. Free/total PSA ratio is not recommended in this context.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is diagnosed with prostate cancer following a transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy. His PSA is 3.2 ng/mL, Gleason score 3+3=6, and clinical stage T1c. He is asymptomatic and has no comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend active surveillance for low-risk localised prostate cancer (Gleason ≤6, PSA <10, T1–T2a) to avoid overtreatment. Radical treatment is reserved for progression.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a PSA of 20 ng/mL. Biopsy shows Gleason 4+5=9 adenocarcinoma, and MRI confirms T3a disease with no evidence of metastases on CT and bone scan. He is fit with ECOG 0. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: External beam radiotherapy plus long-term androgen deprivation therapy
questions.explanation: For high-risk localised prostate cancer (PSA >20, Gleason 8–10, T3–T4), NICE recommends radiotherapy in combination with long-term androgen deprivation therapy (2–3 years).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is diagnosed with metastatic prostate cancer. Staging shows multiple bone metastases and pelvic lymphadenopathy. He has no significant comorbidities and ECOG performance status 0. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: The STAMPEDE trial established ADT plus docetaxel as standard first-line therapy for fit men with newly diagnosed metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with castration-resistant prostate cancer and a history of epilepsy is being considered for systemic therapy. His only metastatic sites are bone, and he is asymptomatic. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
questions.explanation: Enzalutamide is associated with an increased risk of seizures and is contraindicated in patients with a history of seizure disorder. Alternative options include abiraterone or radium-223.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with castration-resistant prostate cancer has been treated with abiraterone and prednisolone, but his PSA is now rising. Scans reveal liver metastases in addition to bone metastases. He remains symptomatic from bone pain. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
options: Palliative radiotherapy to painful bone sites
questions.explanation: Radium-223 is only indicated for castration-resistant prostate cancer with symptomatic bone metastases and no visceral metastases. The presence of liver metastases is a contraindication to radium-223 therapy.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with low-risk localised prostate cancer (Gleason 3+3, PSA 6, T1c) on active surveillance has had a stable PSA for 2 years. At his latest 6-month review, his PSA has risen to 9.2 ng/mL. He remains asymptomatic. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Start watchful waiting instead of active surveillance
questions.explanation: Rising PSA on active surveillance suggests possible disease progression. NICE guidelines recommend re-biopsy to confirm progression before changing management. Watchful waiting is for those with limited life expectancy; radiotherapy or ADT would be premature without histological confirmation; PSMA PET is not first-line in this setting.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with known metastatic prostate cancer on ADT presents to the emergency department with acute urinary retention, severe lower back pain, and bilateral leg weakness. He is unable to walk. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: This is suspected malignant spinal cord compression – an oncological emergency. The immediate action is to give high-dose dexamethasone to reduce cord oedema. Urgent MRI spine is essential but giving steroids is the first pharmacological step. Catheterisation relieves retention but does not treat cord compression; radiotherapy is not immediate; antibiotics are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man on ADT (leuprolide) for locally advanced prostate cancer is admitted with confusion and lethargy. Serum sodium is 121 mmol/L. He is euvolaemic on examination. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check serum osmolality, urine osmolality, and urine sodium
Fluid restrict to 1 L per day
Stop the leuprolide and start an alternative ADT
Give an oral sodium chloride tablet
questions.explanation: The patient likely has SIADH, a known side effect of ADT. The immediate step is to confirm the diagnosis with paired serum and urine osmolality and urine sodium. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures); stopping ADT is not first step; fluid restriction is treatment after diagnosis; oral salt is ineffective.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with non-metastatic prostate cancer has been on ADT (goserelin) for 3 years. A routine DEXA scan shows osteopenia (T-score -2.0). His renal function is normal. What is the single most appropriate next step to reduce fracture risk?
options: Start calcium and vitamin D supplements
Switch ADT to an anti-androgen monotherapy
questions.explanation: Long-term ADT causes accelerated bone loss. For osteopenia with a T-score below -2.5 or if multiple risk factors, a bisphosphonate is recommended. Calcium and vitamin D are co-therapy but not sufficient alone; bone biopsy is unnecessary; switching ADT is not standard; repeat DEXA without intervention delays therapy.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old asymptomatic man undergoes a routine health check. His serum PSA is 15 ng/mL and digital rectal examination is normal. According to NICE guidelines, which finding alone is sufficient to warrant an urgent (2-week) suspected cancer referral?
options: PSA >10 ng/mL in a man aged 60-69
questions.explanation: NICE guideline NG12 recommends urgent referral for suspected prostate cancer if PSA >10 ng/mL, regardless of DRE findings. PSA velocity is not used for referral. Family history and age alone are not referral criteria.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer on androgen deprivation therapy presents with confusion, lethargy, and constipation. His corrected serum calcium is 3.2 mmol/L. Which finding is the most life-threatening in this scenario?
questions.explanation: Severe hypercalcaemia (corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L) is a medical emergency, causing neurological, gastrointestinal, and renal effects. PSA elevation and mild anaemia are not immediately life-threatening. The high calcium level requires urgent treatment with IV fluids and bisphosphonates.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known prostate cancer and lumbar spine metastases presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness and difficulty walking. Which neurological sign on examination is most indicative of impending spinal cord compression requiring urgent MRI?
questions.explanation: Bilateral extensor plantar responses (Babinski sign) indicate upper motor neuron lesion from spinal cord compression, a neurosurgical emergency. Perianal sensory loss suggests cauda equina syndrome (lower motor neuron). The other options are less specific for cord compression.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoes a transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy. Six hours later, he develops fever, rigors, and confusion. His vital signs: heart rate 110 bpm, respiratory rate 22/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg. Which of the following findings is the most critical red flag for life-threatening sepsis?
questions.explanation: Hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg) in the context of fever and rigors suggests septic shock, requiring immediate antibiotics and fluid resuscitation. Tachycardia, tachypnoea, and fever are all concerning but hypotension is the most urgent red flag for escalation to critical care.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer on hormonal therapy slips on a wet floor and lands on his right hip. He is unable to bear weight. On examination, his right leg is shortened and externally rotated. Which finding most strongly suggests a pathological femoral neck fracture?
options: Pain on passive rotation of the hip
Shortening and external rotation of the leg
questions.explanation: Shortening and external rotation of the leg is a classic sign of a displaced femoral neck fracture. This is a common pathological fracture from bone metastases and requires urgent orthopaedic review. Inability to bear weight is common but less specific; pain and bruising are also not definitive.
